learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate an understanding of the role and importance of media buying and planning in the strategic advertising process.
Evaluate the characteristics and strengths of specific media channels and vehicles to reach desired target audiences.
Articulate the importance of advertising strategy and consumer behaviour in generating a successful media campaign.
Gain a deep level of knowledge of the various advertising media available, including features, trends and measurement.
Establish and apply media strategies to achieve strategic marketing objectives both effectively and cost efficiently in response to a client brief.

assessments.task_description:
You will select one (1) recent media campaign and prepare a critical Impact Report evaluating its effectiveness, creative execution, media strategy, and broader societal impact. Your evaluation must be grounded in evidence-based performance benchmarks, contemporary media planning and buying theories and frameworks, and sound causal and ethical reasoning. The report should demonstrate your ability to critically evaluate real-world media decisions, synthesise industry and academic evidence, and draw well-supported conclusions regarding campaign performance and impact.

In preparing your Impact Report, you are required to:

  • Conduct secondary research using credible academic, industry, and trade sources to identify and critically examine the marketing or advertising problem the campaign was designed to address.
  • Analyse the environmental, sociocultural, competitive, and market contexts that informed the campaign’s creative strategy and media planning decisions.
  • Identify and document the publicly observable media channels, platforms, formats, and vehicles used within the campaign. As many contemporary campaigns employ integrated multi-channel strategies, a comprehensive understanding of the campaign’s media ecosystem is essential for producing a robust evaluation.
  • Apply relevant media planning and buying concepts, frameworks, and global industry benchmarks (e.g., WARC, IAB, IPA norms, and channel-specific attention, viewability, reach, engagement, CPM, or effectiveness benchmarks) to critically assess campaign performance and media effectiveness.
  • Evaluate the extent to which available evidence supports claims of campaign effectiveness, identifying any limitations, assumptions, or gaps in publicly available performance data where relevant.
  • Assess the campaign’s ethical and societal implications using media ethics principles, relevant regulatory and professional standards, and appropriate moral reasoning frameworks.
  • Develop evidence-based conclusions regarding the campaign’s overall effectiveness and impact, and identify implications for future media planning, buying, or campaign execution.

A detailed Assessment Guide, Marking Rubric, Impact Report Template, and Exemplars will be available on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission will be via Blackboard. Detailed Submission Guidelines is provided in the assessment folder on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
23/09/2026

All students are required to attend this seminar to pass the invigilated component of the assessment. Students need to have completed the Micro-Credential component prior to sitting the in-class reflection.
assessments.task_description:
This assessment item builds foundational competence in media measurement and responsible data practice. Students complete two industry‑recognised micro‑credentials and an invigilated reflection connecting credential learning to ethical and effective media planning, measurement, attribution modelling, and governance.

There are two components to this task:

Part A - Completion of Measurement Micro‑credentials (asynchronous; self‑paced)

Part B - Critical Reflection: Invigilated, timed in‑class written reflection completed under supervision during seminar time.

A detailed assessment guide, reflection template, submission guidelines, and marking criteria/rubric will be provided in class and on the Assessment folder on Blackboard.
assessments.submission_guidelines: A detailed Assessment Guide, Marking Rubric, Submission Template, and micro-credential course links will be available on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Assessment 3 (Part A): AI-Assisted Omnichannel Media Plan (Group)
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Notebook/ Logbook, Presentation
assessments.task_description:
Part A - AI-Assisted Integrated Media Plan (Group)

Part A has two submitted components: a team video presenting the plan, and a Planning Decision Record.

1.The media plan (presented as a team video)

  • Your plan responds to the client brief and must:
  • Set out a clear strategic response grounded in audience research and insight.
  • Present an evidence-based, brand-safe mix of channels, formats, partner activations and creative considerations.
  • Justify targeting, budget distribution, flighting and phasing, KPIs, and the measurement and evaluation approach.
  • Address ethical implications, ESG considerations, misinformation and brand-safety risks, and responsible data and AI governance.
  • Show planning across a converging media environment, without unjustified over-reliance on any single channel or platform. An unsupported concentration in one channel or partner will cost significant marks.

You present the plan as a video of 15 minutes. Every team member presents a segment, and the segment you present should be the part of the plan you led. The video is marked on the strength of the strategy, creative and media execution decisions.

2.Planning Decision Record

  • The Planning Decision Record is where your team shows its thinking. For each major decision in the plan (audience definition, objectives, creative, channel mix, budget split, flighting, KPIs and measurement, and the brand-safety call), you will record:
  • The options you considered.
  • What your AI tools proposed.
  • What you decided, what you changed from the AI's version, and why.
  • The evidence behind the decision.
  • Each entry names the team member who led that decision and can speak to it. This attribution matters, because Part B draws on it. You are the person questioned on the decisions recorded against your name.
  • Submit the Planning Decision Record with the video. Templates for both is on Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
Week 12 - Week 13

During Class.

Part B is an individual oral exam of about 8 minutes, scheduled across a booking window. A signup sheet and running order will be posted on Blackboard. Orals run in parallel across the teaching team and are recorded for moderation. You must attend your booked slot; a missed slot may forfeit the oral unless special consideration applies.

Detailed guides, templates and the marking rubric are on Blackboard.
assessments.task_description:
Part B is an individual oral of about 8 minutes with one examiner, recorded for moderation. It is secure and identity-verified. No AI or notes-to-camera stand-ins: this is you, talking through your own reasoning in real time.

The oral has two parts:

  1. First, you are asked about your contributions to the media plan, the decisions recorded against your name in the Planning Decision Record. Expect to explain not just what you decided but why, what you rejected, and where you overruled or corrected the AI. Second, you are given a change to the situation and asked to respond to it live. For example, the client halves the budget, or the channel you built the plan around is suddenly unavailable. Walk the examiner through what changes and what holds.
  2. The second move is the point of the oral. It is not a memory test on what your team did. It checks that you can reason with the material when the ground shifts, which is what a planner is actually paid to do.

Because the oral is individual and secured, it is where your mark is genuinely your own. Two students on the same team can earn different Part B marks on the same plan.

How the two parts connect: one thread runs through the whole assessment. You lead a section of the plan, you present that section in the video, you own those decisions in the Planning Decision Record, and you defend them in the oral. Same person, same decisions, three times. Choose your section with that in mind.

Please Note: The oral will be recorded for marking purposes.

AI in this assessment

AI tools are expected across Part A, used as a co-pilot for research, forecasting, budget modelling, scenario generation and insight. The skill being assessed is not whether you used AI but whether you directed it well: where you took its output, where you corrected it, and where your judgement went past it. The Planning Decision Record is how you show that.

AI is not permitted during the Part B oral. The oral is live and unassisted.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to apply for an extension.
